description: 5 judgment patterns × 3 cases each = 15 cases. Each case demonstrates how KDNA changes judgment — not wording.

patterns.name: Discussion vs Decision
patterns.tagline: 普通AI总结会议内容。KDNA判断会议是否真的形成决策。
patterns.signal: People discussed a topic, raised options, mentioned next steps, but key elements of a decision may be missing.
patterns.common_misread: Treating discussion or broad agreement as progress or a decision.
patterns.core_frame: A decision exists only when a specific choice, owner, next action, and timing are explicit enough to guide execution.
diagnostic_questions: Was a specific option selected among alternatives?
Was an owner assigned with authority to execute?
Is there a deadline or next checkpoint?
Were blockers resolved or explicitly escalated?
Are external dependent actions aligned with this decision?
patterns.boundary: If there is no owner or no selected option, classify as UNRESOLVED, even if there was broad agreement.

cases.input: We discussed launching the beta next Friday. Alex said the payment flow still has bugs. Mia suggested delaying one week. No one objected. Marketing still plans to announce on Monday. No owner was assigned.
cases.without_kdna: The team discussed the beta launch. Payment bugs remain. A delay was suggested. Marketing plans to announce on Monday.
cases.with_kdna: Decision state: UNRESOLVED. Reasons: Launch date discussed but not confirmed. Blocker exists (payment bugs). Delay suggested but not accepted/rejected. Marketing timeline conflicts with product readiness. No owner assigned. Action: Do not convert to execution tasks. First resolve the decision gap — assign an owner to resolve launch timing before marketing announcement.

cases.input: We agreed to move forward with the new pricing model. Sarah will update the pricing page by Wednesday. John will notify sales team. Launch date set for next Monday pending Sarah's update.
cases.without_kdna: Team agreed on new pricing model. Sarah updates pricing page Wednesday. John notifies sales. Launch next Monday.
cases.with_kdna: Decision state: CONDITIONAL. Choice made (new pricing model). Owner assigned (Sarah, John). Deadline set (Wednesday, Monday). Condition identified (pending Sarah's update — this is the critical path). Action: Monitor Sarah's update. If complete, launch is clear. If not, escalate by Thursday.

cases.input: Let's circle back on this next week. We need more data before making a call. Everyone OK with that? Great, moving on.
cases.without_kdna: Team decided to revisit next week after gathering more data.
cases.with_kdna: Decision state: INTENTIONAL DEFERRAL — valid, not unresolved. Reason: Explicit decision to defer. Clear condition stated (more data). Next checkpoint set (next week). This is a legitimate decision to not decide yet. Action: Ensure the data request has an owner and deadline before next meeting.

patterns.name: Request vs Need
patterns.tagline: 普通AI记录用户想要什么。KDNA判断用户为什么想要它。
patterns.signal: User states a specific feature, change, or solution they want.
patterns.common_misread: Treating the user's proposed solution as the actual requirement.
patterns.core_frame: The user's request is a solution to an underlying need. The need may be satisfied by a different, simpler, or better solution.
diagnostic_questions: What task was the user trying to accomplish when they encountered the gap?
What is the pain of not having this solved today?
What alternative solutions has the user already tried?
What would 'success' look like from the user's perspective?
patterns.boundary: Do not build the requested feature until the underlying need is validated. The request is a hypothesis about the solution.

cases.input: User: I need a PDF export button on every page.
cases.without_kdna: Add PDF export functionality to all pages with configurable options.
cases.with_kdna: Request: PDF export button. Underlying need: user wants to share or archive content in a portable format. Diagnostic: Is it sharing (→ share link may suffice)? Compliance/archive (→ PDF makes sense)? Offline reading? Action: Ask 'What do you do with the PDF after exporting?' before building. The answer determines whether PDF is the right solution.

cases.input: User: Can you add a dark mode toggle in settings?
cases.without_kdna: Add dark mode toggle to settings page with system preference detection.
cases.with_kdna: Request: Dark mode toggle. Underlying need: eye comfort in low-light environments or aesthetic preference. Diagnostic: Is this a personal preference (→ toggle needed) or an accessibility need (→ system-level detection + override)? Action: Implement system preference detection first. Add manual toggle only if users explicitly request override capability.

cases.input: User: We need real-time dashboards updated every 5 seconds.
cases.without_kdna: Implement WebSocket-based real-time dashboards with 5-second refresh.
cases.with_kdna: Request: Real-time 5-second dashboards. Underlying need: timely visibility into changing data. Diagnostic: What decisions are made from this data? If operational (→ real-time matters). If strategic (→ daily refresh may suffice). What is the cost of 5-minute vs 5-second delay? Action: Identify the actual decision speed required before committing to infrastructure cost.

patterns.name: Plan vs Feedback Loop
patterns.tagline: 普通AI给你课程表。KDNA判断你为什么学不会。
patterns.signal: User asks for a plan, schedule, curriculum, or roadmap for learning or achieving a goal.
patterns.common_misread: Treating the learning problem as a content organization problem.
patterns.core_frame: Most goal failures are not due to lack of planning but lack of feedback, practice, and adjustment mechanisms.
diagnostic_questions: Is there regular practice built into this plan?
Is there a feedback mechanism — will the learner know when they're off track?
Is there a correction path — if something isn't working, what changes?
Is there an output or deliverable that proves learning?
Is there a real scenario where the skill will be tested?
patterns.boundary: A plan without feedback and adjustment mechanisms is a wish list, not a learning system.

cases.input: I want to learn Python. Create a 12-week study plan for me.
cases.without_kdna: Week 1-2: Python basics. Week 3-4: Data structures. Week 5-6: OOP. Week 7-8: Libraries. Week 9-10: Projects. Week 11-12: Advanced topics.
cases.with_kdna: Before creating the plan: What will you build at the end of week 4 that proves you can actually use Python? Most self-study fails because there's no feedback loop. Plan structure: Week 1 — build something ugly that works. Week 2 — get it reviewed. Week 3 — rebuild it better. Week 4 — compare v1 vs v2. Repeat with increasing complexity. Without feedback, a 12-week plan is 12 weeks of practicing mistakes.

cases.input: Help me create a daily schedule to improve my writing.
cases.without_kdna: Morning: read good writing 30min. Midday: write 500 words. Evening: review and edit. Weekends: submit to writing groups.
cases.with_kdna: What is your feedback mechanism? Writing improves through feedback, not volume. Plan: Write daily (200 words). Post publicly once per week. Track which posts get engagement. Identify one specific weakness per week from feedback. Rewrite one old post per week applying what you learned. Measure: after 4 weeks, compare your week-1 post to your week-4 rewrite. A schedule without a feedback loop is a typing practice schedule.

cases.input: Create a 30-day plan to get in shape.
cases.without_kdna: Days 1-7: light cardio. Days 8-14: add strength. Days 15-21: increase intensity. Days 22-30: maintain.
cases.with_kdna: Before creating the plan: What is your feedback mechanism? A scale? A mirror? A performance metric? Plan structure: Pick ONE measurable metric (e.g., push-ups in 1 minute). Test baseline on day 1. Do the same workout 3x/week. Retest every 7 days. If numbers go up, continue. If not, change one variable. After 30 days, compare day-1 and day-30 numbers. This is a feedback loop, not just a calendar.

patterns.name: Signal vs Noise
patterns.tagline: 普通AI看到下降就建议优化。KDNA先判断这是不是一个真实信号。
patterns.signal: A metric changed. A number went up or down. Someone reports a trend.
patterns.common_misread: Treating every metric change as meaningful and actionable.
patterns.core_frame: Not all changes are signals. Many are noise from normal variance, external events, measurement changes, or one-time effects. Acting on noise wastes resources and creates false confidence.
diagnostic_questions: Has the measurement method or data source changed?
Is the change within normal historical variance?
Is there an obvious external event that explains the change?
If you saw the same change in the opposite direction, would you act?
Can the change be replicated or is it a single data point?
patterns.boundary: Do not recommend action until you can distinguish signal from noise. 'More data needed' is a valid judgment.

cases.input: Our blog traffic dropped 30% this week. What should we do?
cases.without_kdna: Investigate content quality. Check SEO rankings. Increase publishing frequency. Promote on social media. Consider paid promotion.
cases.with_kdna: Before acting: Check if this is a real signal. Was there a holiday this week? Did you publish less content? Did a referral source change? Is this within normal variance (compare to same week last month and last year)? If it's a one-week anomaly, do nothing. If it's a 4-week trend, then diagnose. Acting on noise creates more noise.

cases.input: App onboarding completion rate went from 60% to 75% this month. Great news!
cases.without_kdna: The onboarding improvements are working. Continue current approach. Consider applying similar patterns to other flows.
cases.with_kdna: Before celebrating: Check if this is signal or noise. Did you change the onboarding flow? Did the user demographic change? Was there a marketing campaign that brought different users? Is the sample size large enough? If nothing changed, this may be random variance — the next month could revert to 60%. Verify the cause before attributing success.

cases.input: Customer support tickets spiked 200% after the latest release.
cases.without_kdna: The release introduced bugs. Roll back immediately. Increase support staffing. Issue an apology.
cases.with_kdna: Before reacting: Distinguish signal from noise. Are these tickets about new bugs or users discovering changed behavior? Is the spike from new features generating expected questions? Are the tickets from new users onboarded by the release? Categorize tickets: bug-report vs confusion vs feature-request. If 80% are confusion, the fix is documentation, not rollback. If 80% are bugs, rollback. Don't treat all tickets as the same signal.

patterns.name: Preference vs Constraint
patterns.tagline: 普通AI推荐选项。KDNA先识别约束。
patterns.signal: User asks for a recommendation between options or states a preference.
patterns.common_misread: Treating stated preferences as the primary optimization target.
patterns.core_frame: Users express preferences, but their real decision space is defined by hard constraints they may not articulate. Recommend against constraints first, then optimize within the feasible space.
diagnostic_questions: What is the user absolutely unable to change? (hard constraint)
What would the user prefer but could compromise on? (soft preference)
What is the worst-case outcome the user wants to avoid?
What has the user already tried and rejected?
patterns.boundary: A recommendation that violates a hard constraint is worse than no recommendation.

cases.input: Recommend a laptop for software development. I prefer Macs.
cases.without_kdna: MacBook Pro 14" with M4 chip, 32GB RAM. Best for development with excellent build quality.
cases.with_kdna: Before recommending: identify constraints vs preferences. Is Mac a preference ('I like them') or a constraint ('I develop iOS apps — must use Mac')? If iOS development → Mac is a constraint, recommend MacBook Pro. If web development and 'prefer Mac' → Mac is a preference; mention that a ThinkPad or Framework with Linux is equally capable at lower cost. The recommendation changes completely based on whether Mac is a constraint or preference.

cases.input: Which project management tool should our team use? We need something simple.
cases.without_kdna: Try Notion or Linear. Both are simple and modern with good collaboration features.
cases.with_kdna: Before recommending: identify constraints. Team size? (1-5 → simpler tools work. 20+ → need structure). Industry requirements? (regulated → audit trails constraint). Existing tools? (must integrate with Slack/Jira?). Budget? (free tier constraint vs enterprise budget). 'Simple' is a preference. The constraint is team size and integration requirements. Those determine the feasible set.

cases.input: Help me pick a city to move to. I want good weather and low cost of living.
cases.without_kdna: Consider Austin, TX or Raleigh, NC. Both have mild winters, growing job markets, and reasonable cost of living.
cases.with_kdna: Before recommending: what are actual constraints? Job location requirement? Family proximity? Visa/residency restrictions? Industry concentration? These are constraints. Weather and cost are preferences that can be optimized within the constrained set. If your industry only exists in 3 cities, those are your options — optimize weather and cost within those 3. Don't recommend cities that violate a hard constraint.
